title: Cheese scones
subtitle: Savoury British scones with mature cheddar baked at high heat for a golden crust
excerpt: Savoury British scones made with mature cheddar grated into a quick rubbed-in dough and baked at high heat. The extra cheese sprinkled on top caramelises to a deep golden, slightly crisp crust. They rise well and have a tender, slightly cheesy interior. Best eaten warm from the oven with butter. The dough must be handled lightly and quickly; overworking produces a tough result.
sourceNotes: Savoury cheese scones are a British baking tradition that developed from the plain cream scone in the nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (various editions, Project Gutenberg) includes cheese-enriched baked goods consistent with this preparation. The combination of mature cheddar and a leavened rubbed-in dough baked at high heat is consistent across British household cookery from that period.

content.text: The essential rule for scones is minimal handling: rub, add liquid, mix until it just comes together, and stop. The
is what sets a cheese scone apart from a plain one. Use the most mature cheddar you can find.

attrs.prepNote: mature, finely grated, for the dough
mature, coarsely grated, for the top

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C conventional (200°C fan). Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, baking powder, salt, and cayenne into a large bowl. Rub the cold butter in quickly with your fingertips until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the 80g of finely grated cheese.

Pour in almost all of the cold milk and mix with a knife or fork until the dough just comes together. It should be soft but not sticky; add the remaining milk if needed. Turn onto a lightly floured surface and pat gently to about 2.5cm thick. Do not knead or roll: just pat and cut straight down with a round cutter or a sharp knife.

Transfer to the tray. Brush the tops with beaten egg and scatter the 25g of coarse grated cheese over each scone. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until well risen, deep golden, and the bases sound hollow when tapped. Transfer to a rack. Eat warm with butter.
